::: {.cell} ::: # Geom line
![](images/ggplot2_geom_line.png){style="width:200px"}
Line charts are commonly used to display changes of a __continuous variable__ over another __continuous variable__. This can be used to great effect to see numerical changes over time. In this page we will create line charts with `ggplot2::geom_line()`. Through examples we will demonstrate creating: - A line charts with one line - A line chart with multiple lines each representing a different group - A stepped line chart [Tidyverse reference page](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org//reference/geom_path.html) ## Dataset For demonstration we'll load the `fisheries_long_tbl` data from the [mgrtibbles package](/datasets/mgrtibbles.qmd) (hyperlink includes install instructions). We will filter to keep only rows from EU, Japan, Peru, and USA, and the year 2000 onwards. [fisheries_long_tbl details](https://github.com/m-gemmell/mgrtibbles/wiki/fisheries_long_tbl) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} #Load package library("mgrtibbles") #mammal_sleep_tbl tibble for demonstration fisheries_long_tbl<- mgrtibbles::fisheries_long_tbl |> #Filter to only retain rows from certain countries # and to keep years from 2000 onwards dplyr::filter(Entity %in% c("European Union", "Japan", "Peru", "United States") , Year >= 2000) ``` :::
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Single line chart When creating a line chart only one y value can be assigned to each x value per line. To create a line chart with only one line we will plot the information for Japan. Create a line chart of `Metric_tons` (y) against `Year` (x) for Japan. Additionally, we'll set `linewidth=1` in `ggplot::geom_line()` to make the line thicker for clarity. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} fisheries_long_tbl |> #Filter to only retain Japan dplyr::filter(Entity=="Japan") |> ggplot2::ggplot(aes(x = Year, y = Metric_tons)) + ggplot2::geom_line(linewidth = 1) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](geom_line_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-4-1.png){width=979.2} ::: :::
Although you can create a line chart with multiple x values per y values it becomes impossible to read fully. The below example plots a line chart with the __tibble__ without filtering out one Entity or colour grouping by Entity (see below). As there are four entities (EU, Japan, Peru, USA) this means there are four y values per year which are all connected before moving onto the next year. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} fisheries_long_tbl |> ggplot2::ggplot(aes(x = Year, y = Metric_tons)) + ggplot2::geom_step(linewidth = 1) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](geom_line_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){width=979.2} ::: :::
## Multiple lines chart Line charts are great for showing changes across a time range of several groups. Each group can be represented as a coloured line on the same chart. Create a `Metric_tons` (y) against `Year` (x) line chart for our __tibble__. In `aes()` set `colour=Entity` so each Entity will be represented a by separate coloured line. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} fisheries_long_tbl |> ggplot2::ggplot(aes(x = Year, y = Metric_tons, colour = Entity)) + ggplot2::geom_line(linewidth = 1) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](geom_line_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-6-1.png){width=979.2} ::: ::: ## Steps An optional method for a line chart is a stepped line chart with `geom_step()`. This can make it a bit easier to compare the individual values between coloured groups. Create a `Metric_tons` (y) against `Year` (x) stepped line chart for our __tibble__. In `aes()` set `colour=Entity` so each Entity will be represented a by separate coloured line. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} fisheries_long_tbl |> ggplot2::ggplot(aes(x = Year, y = Metric_tons, colour = Entity)) + ggplot2::geom_step(linewidth = 1) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](geom_line_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-7-1.png){width=979.2} ::: :::
